coredata.dc:title: Friction-induced transfer of carbon quantum dots on the interface: Microscopic and spectroscopic studies on the role of inorganic–organic hybrid nanoparticles as multifunctional additive for enhanced lubrication
coredata.dc:creator: Ye, Mengting
Cai, Tao
Shang, Wangji
Zhao, Lina
Zhang, Yunxiao
Liu, Dan
Liu, Shenggao
coredata.dc:description: Inorganic–organic hybrid carbon quantum dots (CQDs) attached the structure of diphenylamine (DPA) on the surfaces (CQDs-N) have been successfully prepared by one-step pyrolysis method at lower temperature. The obtained N-doped carbon dots (CQDs-N) were spherical particles with a narrow size distribution exhibiting photoluminescent property, which are also proved to be an multifunctional additive exhibiting not only antioxidant but also antiwear and friction-reducing performances. Even tested under higher load, CQDs-N still exhibited superior friction-reducing performance till now in polyethylene glycol (PEG) for steel/steel pairs. The function of CQDs-N with polar nature, acting as bearing balls and depositing on the interface to form non-uniform protective film, might account for the excellent antiwear and friction-reducing performances under boundary lubrication.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Inorganic–organic hybrid carbon quantum dots
Contact interfaces
Antioxidant
Lubrication mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface analysis of the worn surfaces

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 21404114 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grand No. 2014M561797 ), and the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo (Grant No. 2015A610051 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) CA, CQDs prepared at 180 and 210 °C, (b) CQDs-N, respectively.
HRTEM image and size distribution of (a, c) CQDs and (b, d) CQDs-N.
UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra of (a) CQDs and (b) CQDs-N, the insert images are CQDs and CQDs-N under sunlight (left) and irradiated by 365 nm light (right).
PL emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of (a) CQDs and (b) CQDs-N.
FTIR spectra of the raw materials, CQDs and CQDs-N.
XPS wide spectra of (a) CQDs-N, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, (d) O 1s XPS spectra.
1H NMR spectra of ADPA, CQDs and CQDs-N in DMSO.
(a) TGA and (b) DTA curves of CQDs and CQDs-N in nitrogen atmosphere.
Antioxidant response and OIT of CQDs-N at various concentration in PEG measured by isothermal PDSC at 180 °C.

(a)Friction coefficient curves, (b) mean friction coefficient and wear scar diameter lubricated by CQDs and CQDs-N (1 wt%) in PEG (load = 392N; temperature = 75 °C; rotate speed = 1200 rpm; duration = 60 min).

Three-dimensional images and the corresponding height profiles (red lines) of wear scar lubricated with (a) PEG, (b) PEG + 1wt% CQDs and (c) PEG + 1wt% CQDs-N, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Friction coefficient curves, (b) mean friction coefficient and wear scar diameter lubricated by PEG with CQDs-N additive at different concentrations (load = 392N; temperature = 75 °C; rotate speed = 1200 rpm; duration = 60 min).

(a) Mean friction coefficient and (b) wear scar diameter lubricated by PEG and PEG with 1 wt% CQDs-N additive under the test load of 392 and 588 N, respectively, (temperature = 75 °C; rotate speed = 1200 rpm; duration = 60 min).

SEM images, corresponding area distribution of carbon element on EDS analysis and Raman point spectra of the worn surface of steel balls lubricated with PEG (a, b, c, d) and 1 wt% CQDs-N in PEG (e, f, g, h).

(a) The image of the worn surface lubricated with 1wt% CQDs-N in PEG. (b) Raman mapping of white box in the (a), of which the change of color represented the size of the area of D- and G-band. (c) Raman spectra of the specified areas in the (c). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Formation process of CQDs-N.
Possible Antioxidant Mechanism of CQDs-N in PEG.
The proposed tribological mechanism of CQDs-N.
Kinematic viscosity of PEG and PEG with different additives.
Atomic % of element on the worn surface of steel balls lubricated with PEG and 1 wt% CQDs-N in PEG.

head.ce:title: Friction-induced transfer of carbon quantum dots on the interface: Microscopic and spectroscopic studies on the role of inorganic–organic hybrid nanoparticles as multifunctional additive for enhanced lubrication

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. No. 1219, Zhongguan West Road, Ningbo City, Zhejiang Province, 315201, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai University, NO.99, Shangda Road, Shanghai, 200444, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inorganic–organic hybrid carbon quantum dots (CQDs) attached the structure of diphenylamine (DPA) on the surfaces (CQDs-N) have been successfully prepared by one-step pyrolysis method at lower temperature. The obtained N-doped carbon dots (CQDs-N) were spherical particles with a narrow size distribution exhibiting photoluminescent property, which are also proved to be an multifunctional additive exhibiting not only antioxidant but also antiwear and friction-reducing performances. Even tested under higher load, CQDs-N still exhibited superior friction-reducing performance till now in polyethylene glycol (PEG) for steel/steel pairs. The function of CQDs-N with polar nature, acting as bearing balls and depositing on the interface to form non-uniform protective film, might account for the excellent antiwear and friction-reducing performances under boundary lubrication.

ce:section.ce:para: Throughout the ages, friction and wear have always been one of the major scientific issues and technological bottlenecks for mankind. It is reported that about 40% global energy are estimated to be consumed due to wear and friction [1]. So far, the most popular and useful way to reduce friction is to use the lubricant with excellent tribological properties. Apart from poor lubricating performance under extreme pressure and high-speed conditions of traditional additives, the S, P, Cl elements contained in the molecules corrode the base metal and pollute the environment [2–5]. As a result, there is emerging research and demand of low SAPS (sulfated ash, phosphorous and sulfur) and halogen-free high performance lubricant additives.

Carbon nanoparticles, such as fullerene, graphene, carbon nanotubes and the like, have been extensively studied in lubrication field and shown excellent antiwear, friction-reducing performance and good application prospects as solid lubricants or lubricant additives [6–11]. The exhaustive literature reviews written by Zhai etc. [12] and Sharma etc. [13] pointed out a widely accepted hypothesis of the friction mechanism of carbon nanoparticles, such as (a) rolling effect [14,15], flipping the sliding friction into combination of rolling and sliding friction; (b) protective film [16–18], preventing the rubbing surface from direct contact in the way of deposition of carbon nanoparticles; (c) self-mending effect [19], repairing the friction surface by filling carbon nanoparticles into the pockets formed by friction; (d) polishing effect [20], polishing the worn surfaces by nano-sized particles. In addition, Luo etc. have revealed in their recent works that friction-induced structural evolution of carbon additives also directly influenced on their lubricating properties [21–23]. However, due to the chemical inertness of the above carbon nanoparticles, it is vital to improve their dispersion and stability in lubricant, which is important for tribological performance [24]. Chen etc. [25] reported that multi-walled carbon nanotubes modified with fatty acid could be suspended homogeneously in base lubricant. Lin etc. [26] modified graphene platelets with stearic and oleic acids achieving improvements of dispersion in base oil, wear resistance and load-carrying capacity of the machine. But it is still difficult to ensure long-term and stable dispersion of carbon nanoparticles in the dispersion medium after complicated and high-cost modification, which is the key factor that restricts the widespread use of carbon nanoparticles in lubricants.

Since accidently invented in 2004, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted large amounts of research for their stable photoluminescence properties, good biocompatibility, high aqueous solubility, etc. [27,28]. Due to their extremely small particle size and rich oxygen-containing functional groups, CQDs exhibit natural dispersion and long term storage stability in polar media and other solution for the controllable functionalized surfaces [29,30]. Based on the principle of friction and the microstructure of CQDs, it is promising that CQDs can be used as lubricant additives playing the role of “ball effect” and “nanometer self-healing". Ma etc. [31] achieved superlubricity by fabricating ionic liquid modified CQDs (IL-modified CQDs) as lubricant material. Wang etc. [32] found ionic liquid capped (IL) carbon dots as additives exhibiting excellent friction-reducing and antiwear properties for the synergistic lubricating effect of IL groups and carbon cores. Our previous work also demonstrated CQDs and chelated othoborate IL hybrid nanomaterials having excellent tribological properties [33,34]. But considering the present research situation that the application of CQDs in the lubrication field is still extremely scarce, it is prospective to design multiple hybrid structures with tunable functions and explore their lubrication mechanism of CQDs.

On the other hand, the development trend of lubrication oil, including extending oil change period, saving energy and reducing cost, requires lubrication oil to have the better antioxidative performance, wear- and friction-reducing property and less consumption, which means that lubricant additives possess a variety of functions and high performance. Among them, the development of multifunctional additives with antioxidation and wear- and friction-reducing properties is of significance and high interest [35]. Cai etc. [36] synthesized three imidazolium-based ionic liquids containing sterically hindered phenol groups as additives in PEG exhibiting excellent friction reduction, antiwear and antioxidant performance. However, antioxidation and antiwear and friction-reducing are all being integrated in one kind of carbon nanoparticles have not been explored yet.

The aim of this study is to design and prepare a novel CQDs (CQDs-N) with antioxidation, antiwear and friction-reducing properties, and to investigate the antioxidant and lubrication mechanisms. The structural features of resultant CQDs-N were examined by 1H NMR, FTIR, PL, XPS, TGA and HRTEM analyses. The oxidative stability of CQDs-N dispersing in the PEG synthetic lube base oil was assessed by its Oxidative Induction Time (OIT). The lubrication potential of the CQDs-N was evaluated by measuring the friction and wear properties of its PEG solution. And the worn surface area of steel ball was analyzed by SEM-EDS and Raman spectra to investigate the function of CQDs-N during the friction process.
The detailed methods of synthesis, characterizations and measurements of oxidative stability and tribological properties of CQDs-N are shown below.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA, 99.5%), 4-Aminodiphenylamine (ADPA, 98%) and polyethylene glycol (PEG, AR grade, the average molecular weight of 190–210 g/mol) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China) and used without further purification.

In this work, CQDs was prepared from CA by a one-pot pyrolysis method. 15 g of CA added to a 100 ml flask reacted at 180 or 210 °C for 30 min under an Argon atmosphere and was cooled to room temperature. After dissolving the solid in deionized water, the few remaining sediments were removed using centrifugation (10000 rpm, 30 min). The supernatant was dialyzed, using a filter with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 1000, against deionized water for over 24 h. The solvent was removed by using a rotary evaporator, and residual material was dried at 20 °C under vacuum for 72 h.

The synthesis of CQDs-N was same to CQDs shown in Scheme 1 . Briefly, CA and ADPA at 2:1 mol ratio were stirred together at 180 °C for 30 min under an Argon atmosphere. After cooling to room temperature, a blue black solid was obtained. The CQDs-N was purified by being dissolved in the anhydrous ethanol, centrifugation, dialysis, removing the solvent and drying.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a X-ray powder diffractometer (BRUKER, D8 ADVANCE DAVINCI) with Cu Kα radiation. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded by a Tecnai F20 (FEI) electron microscope. UV-vis spectra were taken with a Lambda 950 UV-visible spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, America). Fluorescence spectra were taken with a Fluorescence spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Lumina). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured by a FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo-fisher, Nicolet 6700). X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) was carried out by a AXIS ULTRA (Kratos) spectrometer. 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were measured by a Bruker AVANCE III 400 MHz spectrometer. The data of viscosity were collected by a SYP1003-IX viscometer. Thermo-gravimetric measurements were performed by a Mettler Toledo TGA thermo-gravimetric instrument, carried out from 50 to 600 °C with the heating rate of 10 °C/min under the nitrogen flow. The morphologies and qualitative elemental mapping of worn surfaces was taken with a EVO18 scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with an energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) operating at 20 kV. 3D images of worn surfaces were recorded by a Zeiss LSM700 laser scanning confocal microscope. Raman spectra were obtained by a Renishaw in Via Reflex confocal Raman microscopy with 532 nm.

Pressure differential scanning calorimetry (PDSC) test was used to measure the oxidative stability by a computerized NETZSCH DSC204 HP instrument (Bavarian, Germany), recording the oxidation induction time (OIT). The test conditions are as follows: the heating rate, 50 °C/min; the start temperature, 50 °C; the test temperature, 180 °C; the isothermal time, 2 min; the rate of oxygen flow, 100 mL/min; the maximal pressure, 3.5 MPa. The measurement was from adding oxygen to appearing the exothermic peak, the time of which was the OIT.

The tribological properties of PEG with additives were measured with AISI52100 steel balls (diameter, 12.7 mm; roughness, <0.032 μm; hardness, 59-61HRC) by a four-ball tester (Tianji, Xiamen, China). The test conditions are as follows: rotating rate, 1200 rpm; load, 392/588 N; duration, 60 min; temperature, 75 °C. The friction coefficients were automatically measured by a data-acquiring system on the computer, and the wear scar diameters (WSD) of the three lower balls were measured by an optical microscope.

The test data were analyzed and discussed to make sure that the as-prepared product was the target product CQDs-N. Only then the oxidative stability and tribological properties of CQDs-N could be measured and analyzed well.

The CQDs are prepared by pyrolysis for 30 min at various temperatures in airtight environment filled with Argon. No CQDs could be generated at 180 °C, proved by the XRD measurements, where a large number of diffraction peaks belonged to CA derivatives had been detected (Fig. 1 a). When the temperature was raised to 210 °C, CQDs which had a broad peak centered at 30° in the XRD spectrum (Fig. 1a) were obtained, indicating CA had been dehydrated and carbonized during pyrolysis. However, at 180 °C using the same preparation method, CQDs-N prepared by CA and ADPA was an amorphous carbon demonstrated by its XRD spectrum (Fig. 1b) which observed a broad peak centered at 20° [37,38]. It can be inferred that ADPA facilitated dehydration and carbonization of CA, enabled it to generate carbon dots at lower temperatures.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images (Fig. 2 ) revealed that CQDs were spherical particles with an average diameter of approximately 1.9 nm and CQDs-N were spherical particles too with a narrow size distribution ranging from 1.5 to 2.7 nm exhibiting uniform and well dispersed. Combining with the above XRD spectra, there was a conclusion that the synthesized CQDs and CQDs-N are both carbon quantum dots.

In the FTIR spectrum of CQDs-N (Fig. 5 ), the broad absorption band at 3200-3600 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of N-H/O-H, and 1707 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibrations of C=O in amido, explaining the reaction between the amine and carboxyl group. Furthermore, it can be seen that the characteristic peaks attributed to ADPA, such as C=C stretching vibrations in the aromatic ring at 1594, 1515 and 1496 cm−1, and C−N stretching vibration at 1321 cm−1 still remained. As for the similarity between the spectra of CQDs-N and CQDs is that they both have C-CO-O/O-C-C stretching vibrations in ester at 1250-1100 cm−1 and almost all characteristic peaks from CA disappeared, indicating that CA dehydrated and decomposed completely during pyrolysis.

The chemical composition and structure of CQDs-N were further analyzed by XPS and NMR. The XPS spectrum confirms that CQDs-N is certainly composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen. Fine structure spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 6 b) exhibits five chemically-shifted peak components at 283.5, 284.5, 286.2, 287.3 and 288.3 eV attributed to the C=C, C-C, C-O/C-N, C=O and COOH carbon atoms, respectively [41]. Similarly, the three peaks in the fine structure spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 6c) are assigned to N-C-N, N-(C)3 and N-H [42,43], and the two peaks for O 1s are ascribed to two kinds of O atoms (C=O and C-O) (Fig. 6d).

In the 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. 7 ), it showed that the broad shift at 12.49 belonged to –COOH, the peaks at 8.36 and 7.30–6.80 belonged to –NH- and the benzene ring of ADPA, respectively, of which the hydrogen number ratio was 1:9 after the integration. Thus it can be inferred that ADPA had been successfully grafted onto the surface of CQDs and remained the diphenylamine structure with antioxidant, which is consistent with the results from FTIR. Also it can be a support to the proposed structure of CQDs-N illustrated in Scheme 1.

The above analyses of structures revealed the presence of many oxygen-containing functional groups like C-CO-O/O-C-C, C=O, COOH and O-H in the CQDs and CQDs-N. As a result, CQDs and CQDs-N are dispersible in PEG for the similar polarity, hydrogen bond and the minimal spherical structures. Fig. S1 showed stable dispersions of CQDs and CQDs-N in PEG (concentration: 1 wt%) prepared by magnetic stirring at less than 60 °C for 4 h, which still kept clear without any sedimentation after 5 months. This indicates that CQDs and CQDs-N have long term dispersion stability in PEG.
After adding 1wt % CQDs or CQDs-N to PEG, its viscosity slightly increases both at 40 °C and 100 °C (Table 1 ). Thus, the addition of CQDs and CQDs-N do not influence the viscosity of PEG.

The thermal degradation patterns of pure CQDs and CQDs-N are presented in Fig. 8 . It can be seen from the TGA curves that 5% weight loss temperatures are 168 and 187 °C for CQDs and CQDs-N, respectively. In addition, the thermal decomposition temperature of CQDs-N is approximately 500 °C, which is significantly higher than that of CQDs at about 350 °C. These results clearly demonstrate that CQDs-N exhibited better thermal stability.

The oxidative stability of CQDs-N at various concentration (0, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2 wt%) in PEG was evaluated with isothermal PDSC. In order to shorten the test time, the tested temperature was chosen to be 180 °C, and there was a certain time before OIT for 4.6 min to raise temperature and keep the temperature. The antioxidant response of PEG without any additives shown in Fig. 9 a failed to exhibit oxidative stability. And Fig. S2 demonstrated CQDs did not have antioxidant property for PEG. However, after adding different concentration of CQDs-N (0, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2 wt%) to PEG, the OIT increased to 2.9, 5.2, 11.6, 17.0 and 23.7 min, respectively. Moreover, it found that the OIT was positively related to the concentration of CQDs-N (Fig. 9b), indicating that CQDs-N did endow the antioxidant property to PEG.

A possible antioxidant mechanism of CQDs-N in PEG is shown in Scheme 2 . It is well known that the autoxidation degradation of base oil, of which the main component is the organic hydrocarbon (RH), is a free-radical chain reaction. RH will undergo a chain initiation reaction to generate alkyl radical (R·) by heat and light. Then R· react with O2 quickly to generate alkyl peroxy radical (ROO·). It is this ROO· which is the pernicious radical that will further degrade base oil, reacting with RH to generate R· and the initial product – hydroperoxide (ROOH). The CQDs-N containing the structure of diphenylamine (DPA) acts as the radical scavenger. The N-H bond in the DPA of CQDs-N donates its own hydrogen atom to ROO· by the easy cleavage to prevent ROO· from attacking RH.

The tribological properties of CQDs and CQDs-N as an additive to PEG lube base oil at 1 wt% are probed (Fig. 10 ). The mean friction coefficient and the WSD lubricated by PEG without any additive are 0.121 and 0.938 mm, respectively. Adding 1 wt% of CQDs and CQDs-N to PEG reduced the mean friction coefficient by 9% and 75%, respectively. And the WSD was reduced by 34.8% with CQDs-N, while there was no reduction with CQDs. According to the following equations (Eq. (1) and Eq. (2) [44]) of the wear volumes (WVs):
(1)
V =
(
π h
6
)
(
3
d 2
4
+
h 2
)
(2)
h = r −
r 2
−
d 2
4
where d is WSD and r is the radius of the steel ball (12.7 mm), the value of WV was reduced by 82% when 1 wt% CQDs-N was added. Three-dimensional images can be more intuitive to observe the wear and tear of the steel ball. As Fig. 11 shown, the surface of the ball lubricated with only PEG formed larger wear scar along with the deep groove, which was much seriously damaged compared with PEG +1% CQDs-N. It was obvious that CQDs-N (1 wt%) as an additive to PEG exhibited excellent friction reduction and anti-wear property. Consider the influence of ADPA, the tribological property of 1wt% ADPA in PEG was tested shown in Fig. S3. The mean friction coefficient and WSD were reduced by 9% and 24%, respectively. It was obvious that the effect of friction-reducing of CQDs-N was much excellent than that of ADPA, and its antiwear effect was also better than that of ADPA. It was probably that one of the reasons for antiwear performance of CQDs-N was the formation of protective film on friction surface through the adsorption of nitrogen.

Fig. 12 a shows the evolution of the friction coefficient with time lubricated by PEG with CQDs-N additive at different concentrations tested at 392 N. The friction coefficient lubricated with only PEG showed a gradual rise and then tended to stable with slight fluctuation, which could be that it coursed the direct contact between the steel balls under the boundary lubricating condition. When adding CQDs-N to PEG, the friction coefficient curves exhibited a downtrend. Especially when concentration was ≥0.5 wt%, they declined sharply and kept stable at about 400 s. It was obvious that the mean friction coefficient in Fig. 12b decreased sharply at merely 0.25 wt% and then continued to decrease with increasing of the concentration, until increasing to 1 wt% it reached the minimum value. When the concentration >1 wt%, the mean friction coefficient increased slightly but still much smaller than PEG. As for the wear scar diameter, its minimum value was 0.584 mm at the concentration of 1.5 wt%, and the WV reduced by 85%. It can also be found that the mean friction coefficient and WSD both moderately increased when concentration further increases to 2 wt%, which can be attributed to the occurrence of adhesive wear induced by the aggregated CQDs-N between friction fairs [32]. CQDs-N with appropriate concentration was considered to reduce the contact area between friction pairs as ball bearing during the running-in periods, directly accounting for the sharp friction coefficient drops in 100–300 s.

Compared with other lubricant additives in PEG previously reported under the same test condition (a four ball tester, 392 N, steel/steel contacts), such as imidazolium ionic liquids and CuO nanorods, it founds that even at their optimal additive concentration, the mean friction coefficients were all higher than 0.05 [36,45,46]. However, the mean friction coefficient of 1 wt% CQDs-N was 0.03. Even tested under 588 N (Fig. S4), the mean friction coefficient and WSD of 1 wt% CQDs-N were 0.034 and 0.712 mm, respectively, which reduced by 72% and 42%, respectively, compared with PEG without any additives (Fig. 13 ). This friction-reducing performance under high load was much excellent than ionic liquid capped carbon dots (CDs-NTf2) and inorganic-organic hybrid carbon dots [32,33]. To our best know, all the results demonstrate that the CQDs-N exhibit best friction-reducing property until now. However, the carbon nanoparticles of CQDs-N were not tough enough under the higher load (588 N) resulting in the damage of nanoparticles even the protective film, so that the sharp increase in fiction values appeared (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface analysis of the worn surfaces
ce:section.ce:para: In order to understand the role of CQDs-N in the lubrication, the worn surfaces were studied by SEM and EDS analysis. As shown in Fig. 14 c, carbon is uniformly distributed on the worn surface lubricated with PEG, which could be attributed to the PEG. Interestingly, the worn surface of steel balls lubricated with 1 wt% CQDs-N developed deep ploughed marks, and was observed dark areas near some wear marks (Fig. 14f) which was proved to contain large amounts of carbon in the corresponding areas of the EDS image (Fig. 14g). Furthermore, the worn surface exhibited increasing atomic % of carbon from 32.41 to 37.68 and nitrogen from 0 to 4.79 after adding 1 wt% CQDs-N to PEG (Table 2 and Fig. S5). All the results indicated that the formation of deposited CQDs-N in the friction process on the worn surface played a significant role in reducing friction and wear. Micro-Raman spectroscopy was used to further identify the carbon forms derived from CQDs-N on the worn surface. It was found that there was a characteristic peak at ∼700 cm−1 lubricated with PEG attributed to iron oxides (Fig. 14d), declaring oxidation process happening on the steel surface. However, the carbon characteristic bands (D-band and G-band at ∼1350 and ∼1590 cm−1) were detected (Fig. 14h), identifying the existence of deposited carbon. In order to further study the distribution of deposited carbon on the worn surface, the Raman mapping of the worn surface lubricated with 1wt% CQDs-N was obtained (Fig. 15 ), in which the change of color represented the size of the area of D- and G-band (Fig. 15b). It was found that Raman spectra (Fig. 15c) detected D-band and G-band in the red region and its value of ID/IG was calculated as 2.37, indicating a high defect level for the deposited carbon [41,44]. Furthermore, the value of ID/IG of the worn surface rubbed for 30 min was smaller than that of 60 min (Fig. S6), showing the friction-induced structural change of carbon quantum dots additives [21,47,48]. However, there were no carbon characteristic bands in the black region. It revealed that carbon deposited unevenly on the worn surface with disorder and defect structure after adding CQDs-N, which is consistent with results of EDS. The uneven load distribution and the roughness of the contact area could account for the unevenness of the deposited carbon [41]. Furthermore, the peak of iron oxides at ∼700 cm−1 did nearly not be detected lubricated with CQDs-N in Fig. 15c, indicating that CQDs-N can reduce the oxidation on the surface of steel balls during the friction [44]. All the results can draw the conclusion that carbon derived from CQDs-N could deposit on the contact surface to reduce direct contact of friction pairs, contributing to exhibit excellent properties of reducing friction and wear.

Based on the above results of surface analysis, the tribological mechanism of CQDs-N is proposed shown in Scheme 3 . The metal surface generates positive charges during the friction process due to the emission of low-energy electrons from the friction surface. The abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of CQDs-N contribute to absorbing onto the positive charged sites easily and quickly. Thus, CQDs-N act as bearing balls with rolling effect on the contact surface at initial stage, which significantly reduces friction. Moreover, because of the uneven load distribution and the roughness of the contact area, CQDs-N deposit unevenly on the worn surface to form the protective film under pressure, which reduce the direct contact of friction pairs. As a result, CQDs-N as additive in PEG exhibit excellent friction-reducing and antiwear performances.

Most researches have focused on the mono or hybrid nanoparticles as anti-wear and/or friction-reducing agents so far. In this study, we propose a kind of CQDs-N with the structure of DPA attached onto the surfaces of CQDs for integrating more functions in one carbon nanoparticles such as antioxidation together with antiwear and friction-reducing properties. It reveals that the CQDs-N have several characteristics, including a narrow size distribution ranging from 1.5 to 2.7 nm, an photoluminescent behavior, a good thermal stability for being decomposed at about 500 °C. Furthermore, the obtained CQDs-N not only exhibit antioxidant but also antiwear and friction-reducing performances as additives of PEG. The structure of DPA endows CQDs-N with antioxidant property as the radical scavenger. Thus, the OIT of PEG is positively related to the concentration of CQDs-N. The mean friction coefficient and WSD were significantly reduced by 75% and 34.8%, respectively, lubricated with CQDs-N at the optimal concentration of 1 wt% without a change in the viscosity, which was more excellent than that of 1 wt% CQDs. Even tested under 588 N, there still was the reduction in both mean friction coefficient (72%) and WSD (42%). The abundant oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of CQDs-N contribute to absorbing onto the positive charged sites unevenly, and then might act as bearing balls and react with worn surface thus forming protective film, which reduce the contact area between friction pairs. All the results prove that this novel environmentally friendly CQDs-N with excellent tribological and antioxidant properties would be a potential lubricant additive in industrial application. Furthermore, the synthesis and use of CQDs-N as multifunctional lubricant additive may reduce the machining cost and energy consumption, extend oil change period.

Till now, CQDs as new emerging fluorescent carbon nanoparticles have been widely studied and evaluated for different applications, but few in lubrication field. This study was an attempt to approve the design idea of integrating more functions in one carbon nanoparticles (CQDs-N) such as antioxidant together with tribological properties. However, the non-uniform protective film formed by CQDs-N on the worn surface was not tough enough under higher load, causing a sharp increase in the friction curve. Thus, the further research can be extended to improve its antiwear property, endow CQDs with more functions and regulate the polarity for the controllable functionalized surfaces. This would be helpful in developing multifunctional carbon nano-lubricant additives in other more general non-polar lube base oil.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 21404114), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grand No. 2014M561797), and the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo (Grant No. 2015A610051).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Supporting info Supporting info

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.triboint.2018.06.033.
